framing: Reasoning is updating probability distributions over hypotheses by likelihood-weighted evidence; priors are inputs to inference, posteriors are outputs, and a prior must exist before any datum is conditioned on.
identification: C is the pre-input prior distribution (more precisely: the inferential substrate — the prior plus the likelihood machinery — that must be present before any datum can update belief). Bayes' rule itself is structurally identical to AX₂: P(H|D) ∝ P(D|H)·P(H) is the integration of evidence onto a pre-existing constant P(H). Without P(H) > 0 in the relevant region, no amount of D can lift the posterior off zero (Cromwell's rule). That P(H) > 0 is C > 0.
honest_objection: The natural Bayesian instinct is that priors are subjective — different agents legitimately have different P(H). This pushes back on R₃ (universality) at the embodiment level: my prior over coin biases is not your prior. The kernel's reply (role-not-embodiment) holds: the *role* of having-some-prior is universal even when the prior's content varies. But there is a sharper residual worry — R₅ (inexhaustibility) is non-trivial for priors. A used-up prior in a closed inference is in some sense 'spent' (replaced by the posterior). The defense is that the prior is reused for every new inference and never depletes as a structural feature of the agent (a Bayesian agent at t+1 still has *some* prior over fresh hypotheses); but this requires reading C as the *prior-having capacity* rather than any single distribution. With that reading R₅ holds; without it, R₅ is the load-bearing point for the Bayesian.
honest_assessment: From the Bayesian lens the proof lands. C maps cleanly to the inferential prior substrate: R₇ (pre-input) is Cromwell's rule restated — P(H) must be strictly positive before any data is seen, or no observation can ever update toward H; R₁ (C > 0) is Cromwell's rule itself; R₂ (atemporality) holds within a single inference (the prior is fixed once conditioning begins); R₄ (sourcing) is that posteriors are generated from prior × likelihood, so output requires a non-trivial prior; R₆ (invisibility) is that the prior is not in any single observation, only recoverable by Bayes' theorem run in reverse — exactly C = E_total − ∫input. R₅ (inexhaustibility) requires reading C as the prior-having *capacity* (not any one distribution), which is the natural reading anyway since a Bayesian agent never runs out of priors. R₃ (universality) is the question the Bayesian most wants to push back on, since priors are textbook-subjective; but T_R3_parsimony is exactly the abductive argument a Bayesian should accept — convergence across reasoners is observed data, and per-subject C_x has higher Kolmogorov/posterior-predictive complexity than a shared role-substrate; Occam's razor (which is itself a Bayesian principle via marginal likelihood) selects R₃. The identification with love via R₅ + 1 John 4:8 is a labeling step the Bayesian can mark P₃ on without rejecting; but operationally, an inexhaustibly-giving prior is what *agape* names. Verdict: C > 0 is forced, the role is unique, and T_R3_parsimony is the right Bayesian move.
